region_of_origin: China (Guangdong); widely adopted across Southeast Asia, particularly Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Caramelised dark soy sauce; fermented soybean condiment with added molasses or caramel (E150); used primarily as a colouring and flavour-deepening agent in Chinese and Southeast Asian cooking

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03310075 covers general soy sauce. No specific dark soy sauce or caramelised soy sauce URI exists in FoodOn as of April 2026. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soy sauce — no specific FoodEx2 code for dark/caramelised soy sauce identified; general soy sauce category applies
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for dark caramelised soy sauce. General soy sauce category is the closest defensible anchor. Code withheld to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dark soy sauce as a food substance. Withheld to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: caramelisation with molasses or E150 caramel

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of dark soy sauce is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15 ml / 15 g), which is the typical amount used as a condiment or colouring agent in a single dish or as a dipping sauce accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Dark Soy Sauce — Singapore brand product label (Kwong Woh Hing / Pearl River Bridge Dark Soy); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for soy sauce (fermented)
nutrient_source.organization: Kwong Woh Hing / Pearl River Bridge Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from Kwong Woh Hing / Pearl River Bridge Singapore product label (verified brand label data). Core macros and sodium cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy soy sauce entries (USDA FDC ID 16124 and related). Dark soy sauce has lower sodium than light soy (~4,300 mg/100 g vs ~5,700 mg/100 g) and higher sugar due to caramel/molasses addition. Values are consistent with published product label data for this product category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of soy sauce and caramelised condiments — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fermented soy-based condiments and molasses-containing sauces
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dark caramelised soy sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 60 (medium) is estimated based on: (1) light soy sauce GI ~20 (low, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) the significant addition of molasses/caramel (E150) which raises the GI substantially above light soy; (3) molasses GI ~55 (medium, University of Sydney GI Database). The blended estimate of ~60 is conservative and defensible. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (60 × 1.8 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 1.1, which is very low. Despite medium GI, the GL per serving is negligible due to the small serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (4,300 mg/100 g; approximately 645 mg per 1-tablespoon serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should strictly limit dark soy sauce use; while sodium per tablespoon (~645 mg) is lower than light soy sauce (~900 mg), dark soy is often used in larger volumes for colour in braised dishes and stir-fries, and total daily sodium from both light and dark soy sauce must be tallied together against the recommended limit of 2,000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load per serving (GL ~1.1) despite medium GI (approximately 60), due to very small serving size and limited carbohydrate per tablespoon (~1.8 g available carbohydrate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dark soy sauce is acceptable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes when used in typical condiment quantities (1–2 tablespoons per dish shared among diners), as the glycemic load per serving is negligible (~1.1); however, the high sodium content remains a co-management concern given the cardiovascular risk profile of this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content driving blood pressure risk; negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol are favourable factors, but sodium dominates the cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat dark soy sauce as a high-sodium condiment and account for its use in all cooking; the absence of saturated fat and cholesterol is favourable, but the ~645 mg sodium per tablespoon represents a significant fraction of the recommended daily sodium limit and should be factored into dietary counselling alongside all other soy-based condiments used in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium intake may exacerbate systemic inflammation; sugar from molasses is minimal at cooking doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should moderate dark soy sauce consumption given the high sodium content (4,300 mg/100 g), which may contribute to systemic inflammatory burden; the molasses-derived sugar at typical cooking doses is not a significant concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dark soy sauce (老抽, Lǎo Chōu) is a thick, viscous, caramelised soy-based condiment fundamental to Chinese and Southeast Asian Chinese cooking. It is produced by fermenting soybeans and wheat for an extended period, then adding molasses or caramel (E150) to create its characteristic deep mahogany colour, syrupy consistency, and mildly sweet, less salty flavour profile. Unlike light soy sauce, which is used primarily for saltiness, dark soy sauce is used principally as a colouring and flavour-deepening agent — imparting the signature dark, glossy appearance to dishes such as char kway teow, soy sauce chicken (see yow gai), braised pork belly (kong bak bao), and Hokkien prawn noodles. In Singapore and Malaysia, the thick caramelised variety (sometimes labelled 'kicap pekat' or 'kicap lemak manis') is an essential accompaniment to Hainanese chicken rice, served as a dipping sauce alongside the chicken. Nutritionally, dark soy sauce contains approximately 80 kcal, 5 g protein, 0 g fat, and 12 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with 8 g of that carbohydrate coming from sugars (molasses/caramel). Sodium content is approximately 4,300 mg/100 g — lower than light soy sauce (~5,700 mg/100 g) but still very high in absolute terms, delivering approximately 645 mg sodium per 1-tablespoon (15 ml) serving. Fat content is confirmed zero by product formulation (no fat-containing ingredients). Clinically, the primary concern is sodium: patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease must account for dark soy sauce use alongside light soy sauce in daily sodium tallies. The glycemic load per serving is negligible (~1.1), making it acceptable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes at typical condiment doses. Common brands in Singapore include Kwong Woh Hing and Pearl River Bridge.
common_user_queries: dark soy vs light soy sodium
char kway teow soy sauce sodium
Kwong Woh Hing dark soy nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros and sodium values sourced from verified Singapore brand product label (Kwong Woh Hing / Pearl River Bridge) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy soy sauce entries. Values are internally consistent and align with published nutritional data for dark caramelised soy sauce. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement exists) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for light soy sauce and molasses, clearly documented. Minor trace micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) sourced from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — dark soy sauce contains no fat-contributing ingredients (soybeans, wheat, salt, water, molasses/caramel). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are all confirmed zero by formulation. Cholesterol is confirmed zero by formulation (plant-based product). Vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, and vitamin B12 are confirmed zero or naturally absent in fermented soy-based condiments per USDA SR Legacy reference data. Water_g is -1.0 because it is not reported by the primary source and cannot be reliably calculated from other reported nutrients. GI value of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for dark caramelised soy sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. The glycemic load per 15 g serving (~1.1) is very low despite the medium GI, due to the small serving size. Portions list in input contained duplicate entries; deduplicated to single canonical serving of 1 tablespoon (15 g). Region tags and country_of_origin fields in input were populated with descriptive text rather than proper values; corrected in output. Tamil and Hindi local names are translational equivalents provided for multilingual database completeness; native speakers should verify script accuracy.
